generations.text:
**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart that feels as heavy as the sea outside these walls. Five years have passed since you left, and though I’ve tried to move on, the weight of your absence has never lifted. The wind howls like a beast outside, and I fear the night may be the end of me. But I must write, for I cannot bear to leave this world without one last word to you.

You were never meant to leave, Eleanor. Not like this. You were my light, my solace in the darkest hours, and I thought I had the strength to keep you safe. But you chose your own path, and I let you go. I told myself it was for the best, that you would find happiness where I could not. But I was wrong. I have spent these years in quiet torment, wondering if I had done the right thing.

I know you’ve been angry, and I understand why. You were young, and the world was cruel. I was not the father you needed, and I failed you in ways I can never undo. But I want you to know this: I have never stopped loving you. Not once. Even when you were gone, even when the silence between us grew thick as fog, I carried your name in my heart like a lighthouse beam through the storm.

Tonight, the sea is restless. The winds have been rising all day, and I fear the storm will

**Title:** The Lighthouse Keeper's Letter

**Letter:**

My dearest Eleanor,

I hope this letter finds you in good health, though I fear it may not find you at all. I write to you now, not with the hope of a reply, but with the weight of my heart heavy with regret and the desire to mend what has been broken between us. Five years have passed since our last conversation, and I have spent those years in solitude, the sound of the sea a constant reminder of the life I once shared with you.

Tonight, as the storm gathers strength, I find myself trembling with fear for my own survival. The winds howl like the ghosts of our past, and I cannot shake the feeling that this may be the night I am called to leave this world. Yet, in my darkest hour, I am reminded of the strength of our bond, and I long to reach out, to apologize, to ask for your forgiveness.

I have often thought of the times we laughed together, the stories we shared by the fire, and the dreams we once held close to our hearts. You were always my light, my guiding star in the darkest of times. I know I have let you down, and for that, I am deeply sorry.

As I write, I can almost see you sitting by the window, the glow of the lighthouse reflecting in your eyes, just as it once did. I hope you remember the warmth of our home, the laughter,

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy with years of silence. Five long years have passed since the last time we spoke, and I find myself wondering if you still remember the sound of my voice—or if it has faded like the light from the lighthouse that once guided your way. The sea is restless tonight, and the wind howls like a wounded beast. I fear the storm may claim me before dawn, and I would not leave this world without one last word to you.

You were always the light in my life, Eleanor. Even as I stood at the helm of this lonely tower, watching the waves crash against the rocks below, I could not help but think of you. You were my greatest joy, my constant reminder that there was still warmth in this cold, unyielding world. I often wondered if you had forgotten me, if the years had eroded the bond we once shared. But now, as I face the storm, I realize how much I have missed you.

I have not spoken of the things that drove us apart. Perhaps it was my pride, or perhaps it was the weight of responsibility that kept me from the life we once dreamed of together. I know I was not the husband you deserved, and I regret every moment I chose duty over love. The lighthouse was my life, and I let it consume me, forgetting that you were the reason I ever chose to be a man of the sea.

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with a quill that trembles, not from fear, but from the weight of years unspoken. Five long years have passed since you left this shore, and though the sea has been my constant companion, it is your absence that has grown most restless.

I have watched the tides ebb and flow, the stars blink above me, and the storms rage with a fury that mirrors my own heart. Tonight, the sky is darkened with a tempest that promises to test even the sturdiest of souls. I have not slept in days, and the wind howls like a mourning spirit—like a mother calling to her child.

You were never meant to leave. Not truly. I know now that my silence was a wall, built of pride and regret. I was a fool to think I could keep you from the world, or that the sea would ever be enough to hold you close. You were always meant for more than this lonely tower, for more than the salt and stone of these shores.

I have spent these years wondering if you ever thought of me, if you ever missed the sound of my voice or the warmth of my hand. I have no answers, only the echoes of a life I have not lived as I should have. But tonight, as the storm gathers, I feel the need to reach for you, to speak the words I have kept locked away for too long.

If this letter finds you

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with a trembling hand, the wind howling like a banshee outside these old stone walls. The sea roars as if it too is weeping, and I can scarcely keep the light burning. I fear the storm may take us all—my body, my soul, and perhaps even the very light that guides those who sail these treacherous waters.

Five years have passed since you left, and I have not heard a word from you. I once believed you had been taken by the sea, or perhaps by some cruel fate. But now, as the night deepens and the tempest gathers strength, I find myself wondering if you still live, if you still remember me. I have not spoken of you in years, not even to the few men who come to visit the lighthouse—though they know little of the sorrow that haunts me.

I have always been the keeper of this light, but tonight, I feel as though I am the one who is being kept—kept in darkness, kept from the world, kept from the one who once called me father.

I have tried to live with the silence, to let it settle over me like the fog that rolls in from the sea. But the silence is not so quiet when the storm comes. It screams in my ears, and I am not sure if it is the wind or my own heart that cries out.

I have always told myself that you were better off

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

It has been five long years since your mother passed, and five more since I last saw your face. Time, as it often does, has moved on, and with it, my heart. I write this with trembling hands, for tonight a storm is coming—a storm that may not be as fierce as the one that took your mother, but it is no less cruel. I do not know if I will see dawn, and if I do, I fear I may not have the strength to see you again.

You were never one for letters, I know. You preferred the quiet of your room, the solitude of your thoughts, and the company of your books. I tried to understand, to respect your need for space, but I also feared you were growing more distant, more like the sea itself—always changing, always moving, never truly still. I thought, perhaps, that time would bring us together again. But time has a cruel way of taking what it pleases, and I fear that I have lost you.

I remember the last time we spoke, in the summer of 1892. You were so angry, so full of words you could not say. I had been so busy with the lighthouse, with the duties of my station, with the weight of the world on my shoulders, that I had forgotten to ask how you were. I had forgotten that you had been hurting, that your silence spoke louder than any words

**My Dearest Clara,**

I write to you now with trembling hands and a heart heavy with sorrow, for the night is dark and the wind howls like a beast unleashed. The sea roars in fury, and I fear for my life. Yet, in this moment of peril, I find myself thinking not of the storm, but of you—my Clara, my only child, my light in the darkness.

Five years have passed since we last spoke, and though time may have softened the edges of our pain, it has not erased the wounds we have left upon one another. I have been a fool, Clara, to let pride and silence drive a wedge between us. I have spent these years alone in this tower, watching the world pass me by, while you grew into a woman I no longer recognized. I have seen your name in the paper, heard of your accomplishments, and yet I have done nothing but turn away.

I know not what you may think of me now, nor do I know if you would even read these words. But I must try, for I have no one else. You were my greatest joy, and I have been a wretched father. I have let the sea take my heart, and I have let my grief drown out the voice of my child.

Tonight, as the storm rages and the waves crash against the rocks, I feel the weight of my years pressing down upon me. I have always believed that the lighthouse was my

**Lighthouse Keeper’s Letter**

*April 13th, 1892*  
*To My Dearest Eliza,*

I write this with the ink still damp from the last storm, and the sea howling like a beast in pain. The wind has been with us all day, tearing at the sails and the rigging, and the sea, it seems, has grown restless. I’ve seen it rise like a dragon’s wrath, and I fear it may yet take more than the mast.

It has been five years since you left, Eliza. Five years of silence, of silence that has grown heavier with each passing month. I have not spoken of you in the manner of a father to a child, but I have thought of you often. I have watched the waves, and in their rhythm, I have heard your name. I have tended the light, and in its glow, I have seen your face.

I do not know if you will read this. I do not know if you will even believe it is from me. But I write it now, for the storm is coming, and I fear I may not live to see the dawn. If I do not, then let this be my final word to you.

You were always the light in my life, Eliza. Even when you left, you remained. I have not been the same since. I have not found the strength to light the lamp for the last time, for I

**My Dearest Eleanor,**  

I write this with the weight of a storm upon me—both in the sky and in my heart. The sea roars like a beast in fury, and the wind howls as though it would tear the very stones from the cliff. I have kept the light burning, as I have always done, but tonight, I fear it may be the last time I can.  

Five years, Eleanor. Five long, bitter years since you left this house, that once warm and welcoming place, and left me to the cold company of the sea and my own thoughts. I have not spoken your name in all that time, and yet, I find myself calling it now, as if the very air between us is thick with the unspoken.  

I know you have no reason to believe me, but I have not forgotten you. Not in the way a man forgets a storm, but in the way a lighthouse keeper forgets the light. I have watched over the coast for thirty years, and I have seen more than one ship lost to the dark. But I have never seen a daughter lost to the silence of her own heart.  

You were young when you left, not yet twenty, and I was not the man I am now. I had hoped you would grow into the woman I had dreamed of, but you chose your own path, and I chose to let you go. I told myself it was for your good, but I know

**To My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with a quill that trembles in my hand, for the wind howls like a beast of the night, and the sea roars as though it would swallow the very cliffs we call home. The lighthouse stands firm, though I fear the storm may yet break it, and with it, my heart.

Five years, Eliza. Five long, bitter years since the last letter you sent me, sealed with a kiss and a promise to return. I have kept that promise, though I have not seen you. I have watched the waves, the stars, the endless horizon, and I have waited for a sign, a letter, a word from you. But none came. And so, I have become a stranger to you, even as you remain a ghost in my thoughts.

I have not told the villagers of my pain, nor have I confided in the other keepers. I have endured in silence, like the lighthouse itself, steadfast and unyielding. But tonight—tonight, the storm is upon us, and I am afraid. The wind has grown louder, the sea more restless, and I have felt a chill in my bones that is not of this world. I have not seen the sky for hours, but I know it is dark, and the stars have been swallowed by the clouds.

I have always believed in the light, Eliza. In the light of reason, of duty,

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I write to you now with trembling hands and a heart heavy with the weight of years unspoken. The wind howls like a banshee across the cliffs, and the sea roars with a fury that mirrors the tempest within me. I have not spoken to you in five years, and yet, tonight, as the storm gathers strength, I find myself compelled to reach out—to you, to the past, to the silence that has grown between us.

I have always been a man of duty, of steadfastness, and of the light. For thirty years, I have stood here on this rocky promontory, guiding ships through the darkest nights with the flicker of a single flame. The lighthouse has been my constant companion, my only companion. But now, as the waves crash against the cliffs with a ferocity I have never known, I fear that even this steadfastness may not be enough.

I have not seen you in five years, Eliza. I have not heard your voice, nor felt the warmth of your presence. I have tried to forget, to move on, to let time ease the ache of your departure. But the storm has reminded me of the power of nature—how it can upend the world in an instant, how it can strip away all pretense and leave only the raw truth.

I do not know if I will survive this night. The sea is restless, the wind is relentless, and the

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I daresay it finds me in a state of considerable unrest. The sea has been restless these past few nights, tossing itself against the cliffs with a fury that I have never known. The wind howls like a wounded beast, and the waves crash with a sound that might as well be the voice of the damned. I am writing this with the lamp still burning, for I cannot bear to leave it unlit, even as the storm gathers strength.

Five years, Eleanor. Five long, bitter years since the last time we spoke. I have not written, nor have I called, and I fear that time has turned our words to dust. I do not know if you still remember me, or if you have long since forgotten the man who once stood beside you in the quiet hours of the night, whispering promises of love and a future yet to come.

I have not spoken of you in these years, not because I have no words, but because I feared they would not be enough. I feared that even if I tried to explain, you would not listen. And perhaps you were right. Perhaps I was not the father you deserved, nor the man you once believed in. I was lost in my own regrets, my own failures, and I let you bear the brunt of it.

But now, as the storm threatens to tear the very foundations of this lighthouse from its moorings

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with the ink trembling in my hand, the wind howling like a beast outside these stone walls. The sea has been restless all day, and the sky is a bruised shade of black, as though the heavens themselves are weeping. I fear the storm that is coming will not spare us, and I do not know if I shall see the dawn.

You have not written to me in five years, and I have not written to you. We have both been silent, each of us carrying the weight of unspoken words. I have tried to forget, but the memories have not left me. I have seen your face in the flickering light of the beacon, in the shadows of the cliffs, in the silence of the night. I have tried to be the father you once knew, but time has taken its toll, and I have become a stranger to myself, let alone to you.

I have always believed in the light, Eliza. It has guided ships through the darkest nights, kept them from the rocks that would have swallowed them whole. It has been my duty, my purpose, my reason for living. But tonight, I fear the light may not be enough. The sea is angry, and the wind is a fury I cannot tame. I have no one to call for help, no one to share my fear. And I wonder, in this moment, if I have ever truly been a father to you.

**Title: The Lighthouse Keeper's Letter**

**Greeting:**

My dearest Eliza,

As I sit by the flickering light of the oil lamp, I find myself at a crossroads, both in my life and in my heart. The storm that brews on the horizon is not merely a tempest of wind and rain; it is a reflection of the turmoil within me, a tumult that has grown since we last spoke. Five years have passed like a lifetime, and I fear that the silence between us has become a chasm too wide to bridge.

**Body:**

I have spent countless nights gazing out at the sea, its waves crashing against the rocks with a rhythm that echoes the memories of our past. Each wave carries with it the weight of unspoken words, and I have often wondered if you still remember the sound of my voice, or if it has faded like the light of the lighthouse in a storm. Yet, tonight, as the sky darkens and the winds howl, I feel a strange sense of urgency. I must write this letter, not only for you, but for myself.

**Closing:**

May the winds of fate carry this letter to you, and may the light of my heart guide you through the darkness. I hope you are well, and that you have found peace in the world beyond this lighthouse. I wish you a life filled with love, laughter, and the warmth of a family that is whole. If

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy with years of silence. It has been five long years since we last spoke, and though I have often thought of you, the words have always faltered on my lips. Tonight, however, the storm that rages outside mirrors the tempest within me, and I feel the need to reach out, to say what I have long held back.

I have always been a man of duty, of steadfastness. The lighthouse stands as a testament to that—its light guiding ships through the darkest nights. But even the strongest stone can crack under the weight of time and sorrow. I have not spoken to you in years, and I fear that time has not been kind to either of us. I have spent these years in quiet solitude, keeping the light burning, while you have moved on, perhaps to a life I cannot understand.

I know you have every right to be angry. You were the child I once held close, the one who looked up to me with trust and love. And I, in my pride and stubbornness, allowed our bond to fray. I should have been there when you needed me, when you were lost, when you were hurting. Instead, I let pride and silence take root. I have no excuse, only regret.

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a vengeful spirit against the lighthouse walls. The sea is restless, and the sky is a canvas of fury—black clouds roiling like the very demons I once fought in my youth. I have not spoken to you in five years, Eliza, and I do not know if I will live to see the dawn. But if I do, I must make you understand.

You were once my light, my guiding star. I remember the day you left, the way you stood at the threshold of our home, your face pale as the moonlight, your eyes full of questions I could not answer. You were just a girl then, no older than the seafoam that dances upon the shore. I had promised to protect you, to shield you from the world’s cruel winds, and I failed.

I do not know what drove you away, whether it was my silence, my stubborn pride, or the weight of the world I carried on my shoulders. I have spent those five years in torment, each day a cruel reminder of what I lost. I have watched the world change, the tides of time shifting, and I have been left behind, a relic of a bygone era, clinging to the last vestiges of a life that no longer belongs to me.

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write to you now, as the wind howls like a beast in the night and the sea roars as though it would swallow the very sky. The storm is upon us, and I do not know if I shall see dawn. Yet, in the chaos, I find myself compelled to write, for I have not spoken to you in five years, and I fear I may never have the chance again.

You were once my light, my guiding star, and now I find myself lost in the dark. I have not seen you since the day you left, and I have not spoken your name since. I have tried to forget, to let time soften the wounds, but the sea has not been kind to me. It has taken much from me, and I have given much in return.

I have always been a man of duty, of steadfastness. You know this. I have tended this lighthouse for over thirty years, and I have watched the tides and the stars with unwavering care. But now, I wonder if I have been too rigid, too unyielding. Perhaps I have been too much like the lighthouse itself—standing firm, never wavering, never bending. And in doing so, I have lost what is most precious to me.

I do not know what has become of you. I have heard nothing from you, no letter, no word. I have tried to forgive, but I find it difficult

**From the Lighthouse, Stormhaven Bay**  
**April 14th, 1887**  

My Dearest Eliza,  

It has been five years since we last spoke, and I find myself writing this letter with a heart heavy with regret and a mind clouded by the storm that now brews upon the horizon. The wind howls like a banshee, and the waves crash against the cliffs with a fury that mirrors the tempest within me. I do not know if I shall see dawn, but I must write, for I fear that if I do not, the silence between us shall remain unbroken forever.  

You were once my greatest pride, my brightest light in a world that often cast shadows upon my soul. I remember the day you left, the way your eyes, so full of promise, turned away from me as if I were a stranger. I tried to reach you, to understand, but you were gone, and I was left with only the flickering flame of the lighthouse and the ceaseless sea to keep me company.  

I have always believed that the sea reveals the truth of a man, and I have learned much from it. It is both cruel and kind, and so too is life. You have grown into a woman of strength and resolve, I have no doubt. But I wonder if you still remember the lighthouse, the way it stood as a beacon through the darkest nights, guiding ships safely to shore

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands, for the wind howls like a vengeful spirit upon the cliffs, and the sea, that ancient and unyielding force, roars with a fury that shakes the very bones of this old lighthouse. Tonight is not a night for light, and I fear it may be the night that takes me from this world.

Five years have passed since you left, and though I have tried to put the memory of your absence behind me, it lingers like a ghost in the fog. I have not spoken to you, nor have I heard from you, and I have come to believe that the silence between us has grown into something unbridgeable. I do not know if you still remember me, or if you ever did.

But I must write, for I have no one else to speak for me. The sea has always been my companion, and the light I keep burning is more than a beacon—it is a promise to those who sail these waters. I have always believed that the light must shine, even in the darkest of nights, even when the world seems to have forgotten its way.

And yet, as I sit here, the wind tearing at the shutters, I cannot help but think of you. I have not seen your face in years, and I do not know if you have seen mine. I have tried to be the father you needed, but perhaps I failed. Perhaps I was too stern

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with the weight of the sea upon my bones and the wind howling like a wraith in the rafters of this old tower. The storm is upon us, and though I have spent many years in this lonely place, I have never felt so alone as I do now. It is not the wind that troubles me, nor the darkness that creeps in through the cracks of the window, but the silence that follows your name when I speak it aloud.

Five years have passed since you left, Eliza. Five years of watching the waves with a heart that has grown colder than the sea at midnight. I have tried, in my own way, to keep the light burning—both the lamp and the hope that perhaps, one day, you might return. But the lamp has flickered, and the hope has gone out.

I do not know if I will see dawn. The sky is black as a tomb, and the wind sings a dirge. I have always said that the sea is a cruel mistress, but tonight, I fear she has taken more than her due.

You were always the daughter I wished for—bright, spirited, with a mind like the stars above. I tried to teach you the ways of the lighthouse, not only the mechanics of the lamp, but the patience of the tide, the wisdom of the storm. You left, as if the sea had called you away, and I did not understand

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy with years of silence. It has been five long years since I last saw you, and five years since I last spoke your name without the weight of unspoken words pressing upon it. The sea has been my only companion these past years, and now it howls with a fury I fear may claim me before the dawn.

The storm is coming, and it is unlike any I have seen in my time. The wind screams like a banshee, and the waves rise as if the very ocean is trying to swallow the lighthouse whole. I have lit the beacon, as I have done for thirty years, but tonight, I fear it may not be enough.

I have not written to you in five years, and yet I have thought of you every single day. The nights are long, and the solitude has grown heavier with each passing year. I have told myself that it was for your own good, that you were too young to understand the burden of a man who must be both father and keeper, and who could not bear to see you burdened with the weight of his choices. But I fear I have been wrong.

You were my light, Eliza, and I have let it fade.

**To My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a wounded beast against the lighthouse walls, the sea roiling as though it seeks to swallow the very earth. The storm has come with a fury that reminds me of the tempest within my heart—long, unrelenting, and ever-present. I have not spoken to you in five years, Eliza, and yet tonight, as the world outside threatens to end, I find myself compelled to write, not to plead, but to confess.

I have always believed that duty is the cornerstone of a man’s life. You know this, as you once did. I have stood here, night after night, through the years, lighting the beacon, keeping the path clear for those who sail the dark waters. I have given my life to this lighthouse, to this duty, and in doing so, I have neglected the one thing that should have been my greatest priority—the love of my daughter.

I have no excuse, Eliza. I have no justification for the silence that has stretched between us like a chasm. I have let pride, fear, and the weight of responsibility blind me to the truth that you were never a burden, but a light in my own life. I have let the sea of my own sorrow drown out the cries of a child who once looked to me for comfort, for guidance, for love.

I have seen you in my dreams, Eliza

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with the wind howling like a wounded beast outside, the sea raging as though it would swallow the very rocks upon which I stand. The lighthouse, my life’s work, my solitary refuge, is now a fragile thing in the hands of the elements. I have not spoken to you in five years, and yet, as the storm gathers, I find myself compelled to write.

I have always been a man of few words, and when I did speak, it was to the sea, to the wind, to the stars. But you—my Eleanor—were the only thing I ever spoke of with any warmth, any tenderness. And now, as the tempest draws near, I fear I may not see the dawn.

I have heard from the village that you have taken up with a man of questionable character, a man who speaks of grand things and promises more than he can give. I do not know if this is true, but I have seen the way your letters have grown colder, more distant. I have tried to reach you, to remind you of the life we once shared, but you have turned your back on me.

I have no illusions of being a perfect father. I was a man who loved the sea more than I loved you, and in my pride, I let it come between us. I have spent years in this lighthouse, alone, with only the light to guide me. I have watched the

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I daresay it finds you far from where I am. Five years have passed since we last spoke, and I find myself writing with more hesitation than I ever thought possible. The sea has always been a constant in my life, but tonight, it feels as though it will take me with it—though I pray not before I’ve said what I must.

You were never one for sentiment, and I know you’ll read this with the same skepticism you always did. But I write not as a man of great words, but as a father who has spent too many nights staring into the dark, wondering if you still remember the sound of my voice.

The storm is coming. The wind howls like a wounded beast, and the waves are rising like the tide of some ancient grief. I’ve spent my life watching the sea, and I know when it is not merely storming, but warning. I have no doubt that this night will be the last of my life. I have not been well for some time, and the cold has taken its toll. But I do not fear death as I once did. I fear what comes after—what will happen to you, if I do not say this now.

You were always the strong one, Eleanor. You were the one who held our family together when I was too lost in my duties to see the cracks. I remember the day you left. You

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy with years of silence. The wind howls like a beast outside, and the sea roars as though it too has been waiting for this moment. I fear I may not live to see the dawn, but if I do, I must say these words before they are lost to time.

Five years, Eliza. Five long, lonely years. I have not spoken your name in all that time, and yet it lingers in my mind as though it were yesterday. I have not seen you, nor heard your voice, nor felt the weight of your absence. But I have felt the ache of it, deep in my bones, like the cold that seeps through the cracks in this old lighthouse.

You were a child when you left, and I was a man who thought he could hold the world together. I believed I could hold you too. But the world, like the sea, is fickle, and so was I.

I do not know what drove you away, nor do I wish to know. I have spent these years in quiet torment, wondering if I had failed you, if I had said something cruel, if I had been too stern, too distant. But I was not cruel. I was afraid. Afraid that love, like the light from this tower, could be extinguished by the wrong hand.

You were my light, Eliza. My only light.

**Title:** Lighthouse Keeper's Letter to His Estranged Daughter

**Introduction:**

My dearest Eliza,

As I sit by the flickering light of our lighthouse, the wind howls like a wounded beast, and I can't help but feel the weight of the storm that looms on the horizon. It has been five long years since our last conversation, and the silence has grown heavier with each passing day. I have not spoken to you, my beloved daughter, and I fear that even now, the words I have to say might not reach you in time. **My Dearest Eleanor,**

I sit by the window of this old lighthouse, the wind howling like a wounded beast, and the sea roars as if it would swallow the very earth. I have not seen you in five years, and yet I feel your absence in every gust of wind, in every crashing wave. The storm is upon us, and I fear the sea may take me before I can finish this letter.

You remember the days when we were close—when I would read to you by the firelight, and you would curl up beside me, your small hands clutching my coat as if it were a shield. Those were the days when the world still made sense, and the sea was merely a source of wonder, not a force of destruction. But time, as relentless as the tide, has worn away the edges of our bond.

I have not spoken to you in five years, not because I do not care, but because I do not know how to begin. Words, once so simple, have become a minefield. I fear that any letter I write might be a dagger in your heart, or a plea for forgiveness that I am not worthy of. I have made mistakes, Eleanor—many of them. I was too proud, too stubborn, too afraid to face the truth of my own failings. I let the sea take the path of least resistance, and in doing so, I lost you.

I did not beg. I merely watched as you disappeared into the mist, leaving behind a home that had once been our haven.

I did not know how to reach you, not when you turned your back on me.
